role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1024 Graphic Designers 15.4% of wage bill at the 4-digit 5414 level; this is the code's own core occupation — at true 6-digit its share is plausibly 2-3x higher, est. ~35-45%
role_hints.note: Layout, asset production, resizing/versioning, packaging mechanicals, presentation and template work — generative tools already produce first drafts of most of it. The persisting slice is client taste interpretation and brand-system judgment, which sits more with seniors/art directors than with production designers.
Concept direction, brand stewardship and client creative relationships persist; comping, deck assembly and iteration rounds automate.
In studios this small the owner IS the senior client relationship; internal ops automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps 3.7% + 13-1161 Market Research/Marketing 2.0% + account managers in the tail, est. ~4-6% more
role_hints.note: Proposal and content production automates; relationship account management persists but is the portable asset that walks.
Traffic coordination, timelines, asset delivery and versioning admin — near-fully automatable.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9061 Clerks 1.8% + 43-6014 Admin Assistants 1.3% + web/production/junior tail, est. ~15-20% more (interior-designer 18.4% share in the 4-digit cell is sibling contamination from 541410 — exclude)
role_hints.note: Bookkeeping, studio admin, junior production — near-fully automatable.

source_hints.source: AIGA (the professional association for design) research and member surveys
source_hints.why: The design profession's association; its research (e.g. the Design Census lineage, Design POV reports) is the closest thing to adoption/workforce data for this code.

source_hints.source: Adobe creative-industry surveys and earnings commentary on generative AI usage
source_hints.why: Adobe's disclosed Firefly/Creative Cloud adoption data is direct evidence of generative-tool penetration among working designers.

source_hints.source: Freelance/marketplace pricing data (Upwork, Fiverr rate trends for design services)
source_hints.why: The marginal price-setter for small-studio design work; documented rate compression here is the deflation evidence for pi_dist.

source_hints.source: AIGA Design Census demographics (if still published)
source_hints.why: Owner/practitioner age structure; the census's continuation past 2019 is unconfirmed.

source_hints.source: Marketing-services M&A trackers (e.g. investment-bank agency M&A reports covering creative/branding studios)
source_hints.why: Branding/creative studios trade inside the marketing-services M&A market; use for whatever multiple evidence exists — expect thin small-studio comps.

capture_questions: Design work at this scale is billed hourly or as per-project fees benchmarked to expected hours — when generative AI cuts production time 5-10x, does anything stop clients and competing studios from repricing projects to match, given zero licensure, minimal switching costs, and a global freelance marketplace already setting marginal prices?

Who owns the client relationship — the studio or the individual designer/creative director? Retainer-based brand-guardian relationships (ongoing brand management for a client) are the stickiest revenue; what share of small-studio revenue is retainer vs. one-off project?

What realistically stops clients from generating logos, decks and marketing assets with generic AI tools directly — is brand-system consistency and taste enough of a moat, and for which client segment (enterprises with brand teams vs. SMBs who were the small studio's bread and butter)?

Is there evidence of demand bifurcation — commodity production collapsing to AI/marketplaces while strategy-led brand identity work holds price — and can a $1-10M EBITDA studio actually live on the surviving half?
terminal_value_question: Graphic design is the clearest product-replacement case in sector 54: no license, no physical act, no statute — the deliverable itself (layouts, logos, assets) is what generative AI produces. The question is not whether AI cuts studio costs but whether SMB clients still pay a firm at all once AI sits inside Canva/Adobe at near-zero price; the only mechanisms that could hold paid demand are enterprise brand governance, retainer relationships and taste-arbitration. Does the evidence show those surviving as a paid service at roll-up-relevant scale, or is this a terminally deflating base? A researcher who scores this like a normal 'AI-augmented services' code has failed.
special_notes: Terminal-value red flag is the headline for this code: model pi_dist and terminal demand with the product-replacement channel explicit. QCEW-era employment and 2022 receipts predate the full generative-design wave — treat historical revenue as an overstatement of the durable base.

Extreme fragmentation: 13,359 of 15,801 firms are under 5 employees; n_band is 370 with the bulk in the 20-99 class. The acquirable studios are the strategy/branding-led ones — precisely the segment where key-person (creative director) portability is highest; expect heavy earnout structures in any real deal data.
Role-mix contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 5414 — the interior designer share (18.4%) belongs to 541410; scale graphic designers and art directors up when building the 6-digit structure.

Hourly/per-project pricing is a pure deflation channel: fees are benchmarked to hours, competitors are global freelancers, marketplaces already publish collapsing rates — the mechanism for savings passing to clients is direct and fast.

Boundary confusion: advertising agencies are 541810, marketing consulting 541613, web design with programming 541511/541512 (graphic-only web design sits here), commercial photography 541922, printing 323. Creative-industry surveys span all of these; check the frame before quoting adoption or pricing data.
EBITDA normalization: sub-$5M studios report owner comp inside SDE; freelance/contractor pass-through costs also distort margins — normalize before applying multiples.
